title: Government giving ₹5000 Covid relief fund to all citizens
short_text: Viral message claimed government giving ₹5000 to every citizen as Covid relief.
full_text: A viral message circulating on social media claimed that the Government of India is providing ₹5000 to every citizen under a Covid-19 relief fund. The message included a link and asked people to fill a form with personal details to receive the money. Many users shared the message on WhatsApp and Facebook, believing it to be an official government scheme. However, PIB Fact Check later clarified that the claim is completely false and no such financial scheme has been announced by the government. Officials warned citizens not to click on unknown links or provide personal information on fake websites. Such messages are often created to steal user data or spread misinformation. The government advised people to verify news only from official websites and trusted sources. Users are requested to avoid forwarding unverified messages on social media platforms. This incident shows how quickly fake news can spread during emergency situations. Always check facts before believing or sharing any viral claim.

title: ₹2000 Rupee Note Ban in India Viral Message
short_text: Viral message claimed RBI will ban ₹2000 notes, but fact check confirmed it was fake.
full_text: A message went viral on WhatsApp and social media claiming that the Reserve Bank of India will soon ban ₹2000 currency notes and people must exchange them before a fixed date. The message also said that only limited amount could be exchanged in banks and new ₹1000 notes would be introduced. Many users believed the claim and started sharing the message widely. However, fact-check reports confirmed that the information was false and no such announcement was made by RBI. Officials clarified that the central bank had not issued any order to discontinue ₹2000 notes. Similar rumours had spread earlier as well after demonetisation, causing confusion among citizens. Experts advised people not to trust viral forwards without checking official sources. The government also warned users to avoid spreading misinformation related to currency and banking. This incident shows how fake financial news can create panic among the public.

title: Drinking Hot Water Can Cure COVID-19 Viral Claim
short_text: Social media posts claimed drinking hot water and steam can cure coronavirus, but fact check confirmed it was fake.
full_text: During the COVID-19 pandemic, many messages went viral on WhatsApp and Facebook claiming that drinking hot water regularly can kill the coronavirus and cure COVID-19 infection. Some posts also suggested that inhaling steam from hot water or drinking hot water with lemon and baking soda can completely remove the virus from the body. Many people believed these home remedies and shared the messages widely. However, the Press Information Bureau (PIB) Fact Check clarified that these claims are false and there is no scientific evidence that hot water, steam, or lemon drinks can cure COVID-19. Health experts said that the virus cannot be killed inside the body by drinking hot liquids. Officials advised people to follow medical guidelines, wear masks, and take vaccination instead of believing viral home remedies. The government warned citizens not to trust social media forwards without verification. This case became one of the most common fake health news during the pandemic in India.

title: ATM Machines Will Stop Working in India Viral Message
short_text: Viral WhatsApp message claimed ATMs will stop working for 2–3 days in India, but fact check confirmed it was fake.
full_text: A message went viral on WhatsApp and social media claiming that ATM machines across India would stop working for the next two to three days. The message said that people should withdraw cash immediately because banking services might be affected due to a cyber attack and national security issues. Many users believed the message and started sharing it widely, which created panic among the public. Some people rushed to ATM machines fearing that they would not be able to withdraw money later. However, the Government of India’s PIB Fact Check unit clarified that the viral claim was completely false. Officials confirmed that ATM services were operating normally and no such shutdown had been announced. Banks also released statements saying that all ATM and digital services were fully functional. Authorities advised citizens not to trust forwarded messages without verification. People were requested to rely only on official government and bank announcements. This incident became another example of how fake financial news can spread quickly and create unnecessary panic.

title: Government Announces Free Laptops for All Students Viral Message
short_text: Viral message claimed government giving free laptops to all students, but it was fake.
full_text: A message went viral on WhatsApp and social media platforms claiming that the Government of India is providing free laptops to all school and college students under a new digital education scheme. The message included a link where students were asked to register by entering personal details such as name, Aadhaar number, and mobile number. It also mentioned that the scheme was launched to support online education and improve digital access among students. Many users believed the message and started forwarding it widely in student groups and family chats. Some students even attempted to register on the provided website. However, PIB Fact Check later clarified that the claim was completely false and no such scheme had been announced by the government. Officials warned that the link was part of a phishing scam designed to collect sensitive user data. Authorities advised students and parents not to trust such viral messages and to verify information only through official government portals. This incident highlighted how fake education-related news can easily spread and mislead large numbers of people.

title: Petrol Price Will Drop to ₹25 Per Litre Viral Claim
short_text: Viral post claimed petrol price will drop drastically, but it was fake.
full_text: A viral message circulating on social media claimed that petrol prices in India would soon drop to ₹25 per litre due to a new government policy. The message stated that the government had decided to remove all taxes on fuel and introduce subsidies to reduce the financial burden on citizens. It also mentioned a specific date when the new prices would come into effect. Many people believed the claim and shared the message widely, hoping for relief from rising fuel costs. Some users even began discussing how this would impact the economy and daily expenses. However, official sources confirmed that no such announcement had been made by the government. PIB Fact Check clarified that the claim was completely false and misleading. Experts also pointed out that such a drastic reduction in fuel prices is not feasible under current economic conditions. Authorities warned citizens not to trust viral messages related to financial policies without verification. This case demonstrated how fake economic news can quickly create confusion and unrealistic expectations among the public.

title: New Rule: Bank Accounts Will Be Closed If Aadhaar Not Linked
short_text: Viral claim said bank accounts will be closed without Aadhaar linking, but it was false.
full_text: A message went viral on WhatsApp claiming that all bank accounts in India would be permanently closed if Aadhaar was not linked within a specified deadline. The message warned users to immediately visit their bank or risk losing access to their funds. It also stated that the government had issued strict orders to banks to deactivate non-compliant accounts. Many people became worried after reading the message and rushed to banks to complete the linking process. The message spread rapidly across different social media platforms, causing panic among citizens. However, official statements from the Reserve Bank of India clarified that no such order had been issued. PIB Fact Check also confirmed that the viral claim was false. While Aadhaar linking is encouraged for certain services, there was no directive to close bank accounts. Authorities advised people to ignore such messages and rely only on official notifications. This incident showed how misinformation related to banking rules can create unnecessary panic among the public.

title: Government Giving ₹10,000 Monthly Allowance to All Citizens
short_text: Viral message claimed ₹10,000 monthly allowance scheme, but it was fake.
full_text: A widely circulated message on social media claimed that the Government of India had launched a new scheme providing ₹10,000 monthly allowance to every citizen. The message stated that the scheme was introduced to support people during economic slowdown and required users to fill an online form to receive benefits. It also claimed that the scheme would be directly linked to bank accounts through Aadhaar. Many users believed the message and started sharing it across WhatsApp groups and social media platforms. Some even attempted to register using the provided link. However, PIB Fact Check later clarified that the claim was completely false and no such scheme existed. Officials warned that the link was part of a scam designed to collect personal and banking details. Authorities advised citizens not to trust such viral claims and to verify information through official government websites. This case highlighted how fake government schemes are often used to exploit public trust and spread misinformation.

title: Mobile Phones Will Be Banned for Students Nationwide Viral Message
short_text: Viral message claimed nationwide mobile ban for students, but it was fake.
full_text: A viral message on social media claimed that the government had decided to ban mobile phones for all students across India in schools and colleges. The message stated that strict action would be taken against institutions that failed to implement the rule. It also mentioned that students found using mobile phones would face penalties and suspension. The message spread quickly among parents and students, causing concern and confusion. Many people believed the claim and started discussing its impact on education and communication. However, education authorities clarified that no such nationwide rule had been announced. PIB Fact Check confirmed that the viral message was false and misleading. While some schools may have their own policies regarding mobile phone usage, there is no blanket ban across the country. Officials advised people not to believe such messages without checking official announcements. This incident showed how fake education-related news can create unnecessary panic among students and parents.

title: Government Providing Free Internet for Lifetime Viral Claim
short_text: Viral message claimed free lifetime internet for all users, but it was fake.
full_text: A viral message on social media claimed that the Government of India has launched a scheme to provide free lifetime internet access to all citizens. The message included a registration link and stated that users need to submit personal details to activate the service. Many users believed the claim and shared it widely across platforms. However, PIB Fact Check clarified that no such scheme exists. Officials warned that the link was a scam designed to collect user data. Authorities advised citizens to verify such claims through official sources.

title: All Loans Will Be Waived by Government Next Month Viral Message
short_text: Viral post claimed all loans will be waived, but it was fake.
full_text: A message went viral claiming that the government has decided to waive all personal and business loans starting next month. The message created excitement among borrowers and was widely shared. However, official authorities denied the claim and confirmed that no such policy had been announced. Experts warned that such misinformation can mislead people and affect financial planning.

title: Schools to Remain Closed for Entire Year Due to New Virus
short_text: Viral claim said schools will remain closed for entire year, but it was false.
full_text: A viral message claimed that schools across India would remain closed for the entire year due to a new virus outbreak. Parents and students panicked after seeing the message. However, education authorities clarified that no such decision had been taken and schools would function normally with safety measures.
